1) Are there anything that makes bad fodder for discursive meditation? For instance, would it be unwise to meditate on evil exclusively? Is there any harm that comes from meditating unsystematically on themes that are incoherent? Is there anyway that someone could really badly mess up with discursive meditation and how can one avoid doing that?

2) Is there an occult reason why people in the United States of every class and cultural niche seem so brittle? I've noticed that it is nearly impossible to have a reasoned disagreement with nearly everyone I know, and that this cuts across every imaginable divide. It also seems very creepy to me: if we can't have reasoned disagreements how can we have peace and liberty? Is there some sort of magical explanation for why people seemingly cannot countenance disagreement, even polite disagreement?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Not that I know of. I'm sure it must be possible to misuse discursive meditation and end up in trouble, but I've never seen anyone actually manage that.

2) That's a good question that has no simple answer. I think that a world-class case of cognitive dissonance plays a large role in it. Most people are aware that their lives suck, but they've been told over and over again in ten thousand different ways by advertising that their lives are wonderful, or will be just as soon as they buy the latest corporate crap, and the conflict between their wordless sense of how awful their lives are and the babble of endlessly replaying ads in their conscious minds has them ready to snap.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I thought I might take a crack at this, since every time I disagree with JMG some interesting insights come tumbling out:

1) Yes, there are some dangerous topics, but they require almost willful stupidity in order to cause problems in addition to being highly exotic. We're talking the sort of stuff that can't be transmitted over the internet or printed into a mass-market paperback. There are a number of techniques for wringing insights out of an idea or forging a connection to something. Discursive meditation is special because it balances the two nicely and utilizes the natural structure of the thinking process in order to do it. Other techniques favor one or the other results, have higher entry requirements, or else don't have built in safeguards. As an example, while I haven't done extended (or only) meditations on evil, I have done more than a few; the results are pretty consistently that I start to understand the situation and its underlying mechanics, with a few insights in how to free myself from it coming along too. Incoherent and unsystematic themes are actually a great exemplar of the underlying safeguards in discursive meditation. Unsystematic themes are steadily slotted into a larger system (your mind), while incoherence is similarly encapsulated at the level of the logical structures used to deal with them. The only scenario I can come up with in which discursive meditation goes off the rails involves not simply thinking, but actually trying to transpose your consciousness into a different part of the mind/body from normal, and then working from there; which one could easily argue is no longer discursive meditation.

2) Yes. Karmic backlash from a society run by debauched sorcerers. I'm not sure if that is a joke or not.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
2) From my humble European point of view (don't know where OP lives), it could be the agreement I perceive in American culture about living in the best possible country that possesses endless potential - people are inspired to project their highest dreams and expectations as the "base" of their culture, where other folks have a solid "base" - roots on which some expectations can grow and flower and some might not.

In my view, every European country has some kind of cultural discipline that permits venting a bit of your shadow, so you don't get stressed out by all your positive aspirations: etching humour in Britain, rhetorical fencing just for the pleasure of it in France, ritual tantrums in Spain, digestion jokes in Catalonia, a culture of complicated laziness and corruption in my native Austria, acceptance and transcendance of suffering in my mother's native Russia.

Another point might be that when you live in a small country, it's easy to blame all the bad stuff on someone else, or to resort to some kind of cultural masochism. Maybe Americans are overburdened by all that responsibility of being overly great.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) In my experience, actual discursive meditation is a solvent that can dissolve the threats inherent in any theme. The danger comes from imagining that we are practicing discursive meditation when we are not. We all have shadows, which discursive meditation is brilliant at unpacking; however, that is far from an instantaneous process. If we push too far into our shadows too quickly, they will resist being unpacked. Fantasizing that we can still practice discursive meditation once we've unleashed the shadow to run amok offers none of the protections that true discursive meditation does.

Contemplating evil is challenging and disorienting. There is much to be gained from the practice, but it is an advanced skill. Contemplating evil exclusively would be a pretty sure warning sign that one has stopped practicing real discursive meditation and has moved into mantra meditation or, potentially, obsession. True discursive meditation tends to lead the practitioner from one topic to another in a magically intuitive way.

When you ask about meditating unsystematically on incoherent themes, are you still asking about discursive meditation? (I consider discursive meditation to be a very systematic practice, and thus quite safe.) If, by meditating unsystematically, you mean moving away from discursive meditation, then incoherent themes could be terribly captivating and glamorizing. Their incoherency helps them to sneak around the gatekeepers of the ego and superego, taking root deep down where they grow and fester unnoticed, like most advertising does.

For example, the inconsistent and inherently incompatible protocols governing approved behaviors during the Covid panic have overloaded the mental coherency of far too many of the people who tried to reflect on them exclusively as truths and dogma. That too is similar to mantra meditation or obsession. Discursive meditation allows and requires considering the possibility that those protocols could be power grabs, political expedients, or religious incantations, as well as truths or dogma, as well as lies or tricks. I have found Covid protocols to offer an amazingly incoherent theme that it has proven quite beneficial and protective to contemplate discursively.

The only incoherent themes I would handle with kid gloves are void, emptiness, dissolution, the penumbra. As Dion Fortune pointed out, becoming captivated by them leads to a messy and tragic end. Madness could be considered a mind getting lost in the void. They are still worth contemplating discursively, but always carefully, and only in small doses.

— Christophe

comments.title: Texts of Classical religion
comments.content: Other than Homer and Hesiod, which are the most important texts of Greek, and also Roman, paganism / polytheism? Which Classical philosophers and other writers had much to say about the gods?

replies.title: Re: Texts of Classical religion
replies.content: You'll want to talk to a Classical Pagan about that. I have a good general knowledge of the Neoplatonists and Stoics but I'm not a Classics scholar in any broader sense.

replies.title: Re: Texts of Classical religion
replies.content: I'm not a Classics scholar or Pagan but I'm currently reading Cicero's "On the Nature of the Gods," which is pretty interesting.  I've also seen some folks recommend the surviving writings of Emperor Julian, last of the Pagan Roman Emperors.  He produced a number of written workings that defended and explicated Paganism toward the end of the Western Roman Empire.

replies.title: Re: Texts of Classical religion
replies.content:
Not a classics scholar but I have a decent grounding in it.

Politics and religion were deeply intertwined in that time period. Works dealing with "secular" topics would be seen as having religious overtones in their original context. As an example, if you want to really understand Jupiter, you have to understand the roman legal system.

Cicero is great. For the reasons above, anything by him is highly recommended.

Julian is quite nice but does come with a warning: The roman religious systems had become royally messed up by the time he came around, and was explicitly trying to reinterpret everything in a neoplatonic framework. As long as you know his biases and don't misunderstand his view of religion for the older substrate, his work can be quite illuminating.

Plutarch and Sallust are the other two writers I would add to the list.

Ovid is a major figure, and to my mind, also a major donkey. There is a lot of his writing extant, and it includes a great deal of material. But it is worth remembering that his work was seen as impious when he was alive. Anything from him should be taken with an entire spoonful of salt.

replies.title: Re: Texts of Classical religion
replies.content: Ovid was an urban, and urbane, skeptic. He did not believe in the myths, he was just relaying them. I hear some modern commentators feel Ovid was sympathetic to the people who believed them, but his contemporaries saw through him.

replies.title: Re: Texts of Classical religion
replies.content:
RE: Ovid, I am of the opinion that the mere act of relaying the stories of the gods is looked askance at, though one can serve as a vessel for the gods without knowing it.

Much of the fear and concern of Ovid seems due to some perception that there is some "pure" tradition that he is muddying (disregarding the variegated and sometimes incongruous landscape of the original Hellenic "texts," as it were). His chief contribution, besides the retelling, is in my view the concept of metamorphoses: that although the gods themselves are immutable, the symbols and vehicles in which they manifest themselves change. In light of the fact that, with rare exceptions perhaps, there are no unbroken Romano-Hellenic traditions, this idea of change is important, especially as it permitted the syncretism of the Renaissance, which permitted the "survival" of the gods during periods of vehement monotheism. Indeed, the "reconstitution" of older traditions is, in this light, merely part of the dynamic.

One is as apt to find the gods expressing themselves in the divine poets as in the compendiums of mythographers as on the back of a cereal box as in the contemplation of an oak stirring in the breeze. The path is wherever you start.

Axé,
Fra' Lupo

replies.content: Beginning musician here: Could you explain what you mean with visualizing  the music? Do you visualize the bars while playing?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I visualized the patterns and saw and felt the shape of the patterns. I felt the rhythm and the ‘feeling of the music’ in my body as it came out of me. The only way I can break this down in language right now is that I was able to sort of project my knowing-feeling into the music, and feel it into my fingers, my breath, and my sense of being with the other musicians, if I was playing with any at the time.

Visualizing and feeling into the patterns is simpler with folk music that is in the form of jigs, reels, polkas, etc. But if you know a classical piece well - for instance - if you listen to it, and play around with your senses, it should be possible to break it down into chunks.

I don’t know if any of this makes sense to people other than me - I am strongly visual and kinesthetic, so feeling and visualizing into things became instinctive for me.

If you have other strengths, my suggestion would be to certainly try this (carefully and gently at first, as if you were starting to learn discursive meditation), but to also think about how your own strengths might help you feel into the music and your body’s building muscle memory.

Here is a bit of lesson that might provide you with a place to start.

If you learn by ear, then listen to the piece you want to learn, and internalize it (by this, I mean to know it so well that you can, if this is a strength, ‘play’ it in your head). Then feel into it as you try to work it out with your fingers (and breath or bowing or whatever) and try to start picking up phrases, and find whatever visual imagery makes sense to you.

If you learn by notation or letters, you can try listening to the piece and follow along while looking at your music as a first step before the rest. I did both methods and found them helpful in different circumstances.

All that said, I have no idea whether this will be actually helpful to you in learning as a beginning musician. When I used to teach beginners, I started with technique and the notes - this sort of stuff never came up because it was something I just did, and not anything that I’d thought anyone might want to hear about at the time. So please don’t frustrate yourself trying this.

If it were me, I’d certainly explore this, but also listen very carefully to what your teacher has to say to guide you. Follow that advice. And then practice, practice, practice.

comments.title: CosDoc second readings / meanings
comments.content:
Hello, Mr. Greer.

There is something that is bugging me for a while. Take, for example, this CosDoc paragraph:

--
As we have already seen, a Great Entity projects its concept
of itself. This, however, is not the beginning of objectivisation in
a universe, though dealt with first in the order of instruction in
order to make that which follows more comprehensible; for the
Great Entity, when it projected the image of itself, gained substance
of the same nature as the projection, awaiting the organisation
which that projection should bring about.
--

A first read, within context, is talking about a Great Entity which makes a copy of itself out of cosmic matter, it projects the thought and the matter will come. It creates an objective image which is what other consciences around will interact with. In practical terms directly deduced from the metaphore, if I wanted other people to interact differently with me, then I have to change the image I am projecting to the world, so people see a different me. Powerful psychological stuff.

Then you can make a second read, out of context. It tells how a Great Entity can build something great (it's a great entity, so whatever it creates should be great, isn't it?). First it must make plans (projections), only after the design is made, it should go on with gathering materials for the work as instructed in the plans (of the same kind of the work you want to make), and finally organise the materials to complete the plans. They look like very rough instructions for a successful great work.

It's hard to get to the second meaning, since the meaning that comes from context obscure our thinking. I think this is what we usually call 'second readings'. In common talking, it's very easy to get misunderstandings when the same set of words can be understood in two or three different ways. Very often, what a person says can mean different things, and we usually pick the right meaning from context, but sometimes it doesn't work. Sometimes we are not even aware that there are second meanings.
So, studying this book is, in a way, developing the ability to see second meanings, since I am becoming more aware of them (and I am terrible at doing second readings, mind you).

So far, so good.

What is bugging me, it's that now, the very first thing that I attempt when I study a paragraph is to find second meanings. You instructed to study the text with the images it suggested in mind, but I have failed to do so for several chapters. What I've been doing instead was to take a broad look over a set of keywords, and trying to make another sense of them, if put in a different context, with different degrees of success. In some places I think Fortune was talking about movie films, the chemical reactions that produce images in the film, where rays can pass through the parts of the film that have reacted, the frecuency you have to move the rings in order to see the movie correctly, and how the film is stored in a disc and can be carried away. In other places it looks as if she's talking about gestating babies. In other sections, I'd swear she's talking about how the parliament dictates civil laws.

I was so absorbed with this game, that I got lost of how Fortune moved from 'Great Organisms' to 'Great Entities'. It appears that she is talking about the same thing: traveling atoms that have settled in their right orbits. I can't tell the difference. In Spanish, an organism is an alive thing that has 'organs', parts that perform different tasks, while an entity is an association of people, or just a being, not necessarily alive. If I were using these different words for the same concept, I guess Organism is to be applied to the Administration, while Entities is the same thing but in the Corporate/Private sector. But other than this, I can't find nice second meanings in these chapters.

I guess I should go back and repeat the study process, this time holding onto the images.


On a second but related issue, I'm slowly reading 'Dogme et rituel de la haute magie', and although I have not done yet a serious study, I can't really find second meanings in this text. There are some hints at practical magic here and there, without really saying, but nothing like the multiple layer of meanings that is CosDoc. Is 'Dogme et Rituel' really easier, or is it just even more occult?

replies.title: Re: CosDoc second readings / meanings
replies.content: Okay, good. You've caught onto one of the traps hardwired into occult literature. Since the Cos.Doc. requires many readings, you have time to explore it in many different ways. As for Dogme et Rituel, it's a much simpler book -- it doesn't have the nested meanings, though it's full of hints that take meditation to unfold. Lévi was writing for a general audience, not for a circle of senior initiates, as Fortune was.

comments.title: Cult of Bitcoin
comments.content:
Almost two weeks ago I was listening to a podcast featuring a true-believing Bitcoin maxi who brought up a bunch of points about how decentralized crypto can defund forever wars, tame predatory bankers, and most importantly stop a bunch of bureaucrats from stealing money from my savings account with inflation. The next day Elon started shooting his mouth off and the price began to slide, so I decided to learn more and see if this is a worthy investment at a discount or just a collapsing bubble.

I do not understand the nuances of the code, but the following 10 days of nonstop attacks from legacy media, China, and even the Pope convinced me that this must be a dangerous revolutionary technology worth a second look. The natal chart appears to have been elected with that exact purpose in mind, leading some to speculate that the mysterious founder had some astrological chops.

https://astroinsightsblog.wordpress.com/2021/01/25/bitcoin-comes-of-age/

Finding out about the upcoming eclipse square with Saturn and Uranus made me feel queazy about the money I put down, but by the time I learned about that it was already too late and the fever had swept me down river. Has there ever been a speculative asset before where the investors would rather lose all their money than surrender and capitulate? It occurred to me that as someone brought up in Christianity this made me feel like I was living in the book of Exodus, where Satoshi is Moses leading us out of debt slavery, delivering the blockchain tablets to follow, and mysteriously disappearing along the way after infighting broke out.

Twitter is where we gather for inspiration, and oddly enough the hashtag is full of religious iconography with even Kek making multiple appearances (my favorite is the haloed version with a deeply sympathetic face supporting a struggling Hodler). Faith is rewarded, weakness is despised, and "to the moon" sounds a whole lot like the promised land. Alt coins are the Levantine false gods trying to lead the chosen people astray, and Pharaoh FUD's army is still hot on our heels.

Now when facing establishment pundit's accusations of being a cult some of the more prominent maxis have actually started deliberately embracing it, as if to say "Hell yeah, if being in a cult means believing in something bigger than the next quarter in a dying financial system then we are a cult!"

The effect on me was profound as I have never invested in anything in my life that I cannot hold in my own hands. It was the promise of financial gain mixed with a sense of purpose that kept me up at night obsessively researching. I realized that I was in an altered state and asked my wife to remind me to not put more down than we are prepared to lose. It would suck if it went to zero (or the grid failures win out sooner than expected) but at least it meant I resisted slavery and did more than just go along to get along. In that respect I am far less fearful than normal.

After a modest amount of occult training it is clear than something more is at work here and I wish to understand it better. Even if turns out to be a good call it still bothers me that I did not recognize what was going on it at first and found myself under the influence.

replies.title: Re: Cult of Bitcoin
replies.content: As you research these currencies, I would really like to recommend you look at the energy consumption requirements of bitcoin in particular and the ecological implications of that. As far as I can tell (though I am no expert), all the cryptocurrencies fundamentally rely on an increasing rate of consumption of some resource to function. That is, for a fixed number of people making a fixed rate of transactions with a fixed technology, the rate of energy use of bitcoin, for instance, will continue to grow without bound. "Proof of work" currencies consume energy as their resource (remember in physics work = energy), while "proof of space" currencies consume disk drives and therefore physical resources (rare minerals, etc.) If there *is* something occult going on with them, it's far from clear to me it's ethically wholesome.

replies.content:

The best way I've found to close (though I am strongly influenced by Japanese culture here) is to bow, and then take a step back, ending with my feet shoulder width apart (they are usually close together if I am standing for prayer).

0) You may simply wish to ask your gods what they prefer and/or want. You're already talking to them, why not hammer out the details of etiquette.

1) I can't speak to Anat, but Mithras and the rest of the Persian deities usually had their prayers closed with a word (I can't remember what it originally was) rendered as "satisfaction" in English. "To Fravashi, my guide, the giver of light; satisfaction." Being a common devotion. To my knowledge, most cultures have an entire closing formula for a prayer rather than a single word. Zoroastrians would recite the Ashem Vohu three times after a session of devotion. "So mote it be" is an old one that was merely inherited by neopagans.

2) It really depends. Hermes seemed delighted by the attendant irony to that. It's never felt appropriate with another deity. The gods can be quite patient with human idiocy, and most of us really don't know any better. This feeds back into point 0.

comments.title: Western Ceremonial Magic End Goal
comments.content:
When ceremonial magic is used for reaching the higher-self, what actually is this higher-self? Would the end goal be considered enlightenment (although not necessarily in a Nirvana or Eastern sense)?

I ask as I've been looking more into Western ceremonial magic (hermetic / Golden Dawn and your CGD) and trying to understand how the end goal compares to Eastern paths (yoga, vipassana, etc. (practiced in their cultural context and not heavily boiled down, I.e not just doing mindfulness meditation)).

Thank you!

-John

replies.title: Re: Western Ceremonial Magic End Goal
replies.content:
Okay, there are actually two "higher selves." The Golden Dawn tradition calls them the Lower Genius and the Higher and Divine Genius; Dion Fortune calls them the Individuality and the Divine Spark -- well, there are plenty of names, because occult philosophers like to coin their own jargon. The Individuality or Lower Genius is the self that endures from life to life; in most of us, it's basically asleep, and your present incarnation is its dream life. When it wakes up, the result is rather like lucid dreaming: you become aware of who you actually are, who you were (as the Zen masters like to say) before your mother and father were born, and you can draw on the memories, talents, and capacities of all your lives, not just this one.

The Divine Spark or Higher and Divine Genius is something considerably more robust. It is the basic pattern of yourself, the full set of potentials you have in you, not just in this life but in all lives. The Individuality is evolving to fulfill the potentials of the Divine Spark, just as your Personalities (the selves you think you have in the dream we call being alive) are each trying to express your Individuality. So the closer you get to attunement with your Divine Spark, the more of the tremendous power and potential you were created to express comes through -- and that reaches far beyond what we usually think of as human potential.

We can understand a little of what it means to awaken into our Individualities. We can't even begin to grasp what it means to awaken into our Divine Sparks. Enlightenment -- well, that word means a lot of different things, but it can certainly be applied to the first of these. The latter? "Transfiguration" is about as close as we can get.

replies.title: Re: Western Ceremonial Magic End Goal
replies.content:
Is there a way to laser-focus ourselves on our Divine Spark, or is this a shortcut we shouldn't take because the path through our Individualities is crucial to being able to do this?

And, are there other ways than magic to efficiently do the work of fully developing our Individuality?

replies.title: Re: Western Ceremonial Magic End Goal
replies.content:
1) It's a shortcut you can't take. Only when your Individuality has become fully awake can it begin the process of consciously aligning with the Divine Spark. It's important to let these processes unfold; you can't make the wheat grow any faster by pulling up each shoot!

2) All of existence is part of the way that your Individuality awakens. Magic is a part of that well suited to some people.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
A few questions about how karma works:

1. If someone has bad karma coming their way, does a certain amount of bad stuff have to happen to them and the universe will keep throwing misfortune at them until it has? Or do they just need to have a certain amount of potentially bad stuff thrown their way? Then if they handle it so well very little bad gets through, the account is still considered settled?

2. You've said one of the ways to burn off karma is to realise the full consequences of your actions on the astral plain. Is this limited by your current incarnation's knowledge and empathy? Or can you also clear actions where you didn't know you did anything wrong, or even from a previous life you don't remember?

3. According to occuly philosophy, souls have to experience pretty much every bad thing as part of their development. What happens if a soul needs to experience something but either doesn't have bad enough karma to bring it on, or they have enough karma but it's for a different specific kind of bad? Is the learning experience put on hold until they've done the right kind of wrong to earn it?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Former Buddhist with about a decade of fairly serious practice here:

1) Karma is simply action, and by extension, causality. It's not a cosmic checking account that has to be balanced out down to the cent. Actions have consequences, harmful ones increase the amount of harm in the world, benevolent ones increase the amount of benevolence in the world; and in either case, the person doing things is in the middle of that change. The most common understanding of "bad karma" is that it is forcing you to realize that you are not separate from others, and the resulting negative events are to help overcome the illusion of separation. It is possible to handle things so well that relatively little suffering arises from unwholesome actions, this is usually called "skillful means". Your next question makes it clear you are familiar with at least some of how it works.

2) This actually varies widely. Usually, all your karma is present in your current incarnation even if you don't remember your past and aren't required to deal with all of it at once. In this case, the distinction between past and current incarnation as a source of karma isn't terribly valuable, you are still dealing with the same karma and the same patterns and modes of being. Sometimes, past lives become the spiritual equivalent of a cyst; they are a minor but chronic problem until they are removed, then they temporarily turn into a large problem until they are dealt with.

3) The common western formula, as I'm aware of it is "The soul must know and experience all things". My understanding is that this doesn't mean every bad thing has to happen to you, it means that the soul needs some sort of balance and communion with everything it is in contact with. Once it does that, it is allowed into a wider circle for new lessons, and so on.

An important addendum, the Buddha himself taught that trying to uncover the workings of karma is a barrier to enlightenment. The thing to focus on is restraining your own vices and cultivating your own virtues; growing in understanding and allowing the three poisons to fall away.

If you are trying to puzzle out suffering in your own life, you might be better served by asking a more specific questions. Also, the FAQ has some really good techniques in it, and the books recommended are top notch as well. Hopefully this helps.

It's a matter of getting hit with the consequences of your actions until you get a clue and outgrow the aspects of your character that caused the problems. That can be fast, it can be slow, it can involve a lot of fancy footwork on your part, but until you grow up, it just keeps on coming.

Several days ago as part of our ongoing friendship I offered the tree the tobacco seedlings that I had not planted in the garden, which I am growing for use in making offerings in nature. Around that same time I also directed the blessing from a GCC communion ritual especially toward the same tree. A day or two later, around 9pm, I heard a cracking sound that I recognized as a large tree branch separating from a tree. It took some time before I finally heard the thud of the large end hitting the ground. Then my husband and I went outside to investigate. We found that the limb had fallen off this same tree. Incredibly, the limb aligned almost exactly up and down along the driveway for the house next door, which is on the other side of the tree from the property line, the one spot where it would do no damage to the houses or to any of the trees and shrubs nearby. This was no small limb, either; my husband measured the end that separated from the tree as 11.5 inches in diameter! Plus it fell just about 12 hours away from when I usually commune with the tree, around 9am, and where I stand against the tree was not blocked by any portion of the fallen limb while some of the foliage did block the trunk next to that area.

Here is my question: does it seem likely to you, as it does to me, that the limb falling as it did was not a coincidence, that the tree intended it to fall as it did, so that it caused no damage and made it easy for my husband to salvage the wood for our wood stove? He spent the next two mornings doing so, and I made a small gift to the tree as well.

replies.title: Re: A gift from a tree?
replies.content: Sounds like a generous act to me. The tree doubtless knew that the branch was going to go, and made sure that it went in the safest possible way.

replies.title: Re: A gift from a tree?
replies.content:
I have always thought plants have souls and strongly suspect they think, though not in the way we do. It’s known they “talk” to each other with pheromones.

That being the case, this seems like an excellent time to air my long-held theory that, properly understood, science and religion are two sides of the same coin. This has not been a popular idea in the U.S., where I live, for the last 50 years. I feel I will be vindicated, though!

replies.title: Re: A gift from a tree?
replies.content: Certainly science and religion are two ways of talking about the same universe, and should be saying compatible things -- when they don't, one or both of them have gotten confused. Lévi talks about that, curiously enough.

replies.title: Re: A gift from a tree?
replies.content:
We had a towering 100-year old pin oak in our back yard, positioned exactly in the center of the entire block on which our house stands. It was one of a few reasons why we bought that house. (Another was all the windows it had, and the sunlight they let in: much more glass than is usual for comfort during a frozen New-England winter.)

The tree became infested with racoons, and there turned out to have been serious decay where the largest branch joined the trunk. One rainy morning when I was sitting at an upstairs window, I heard a crack and saw an enormous wall of green rushing straight at me, followed by a ground-shaking thud. It was the branch coming down, and it crushed our entire back yard beneath it. If fell at just the right angle that it did not break the window and go right through my own chest, and it landed right between our house and our neighbor's. At that time of day my wife would normally have been outside in the yard, right where the tree fell, but it was rainy and she had happened to come inside just then.

So we arranged for the fallen limb to be cut up, and then the seriously damaged tree to be taken down. We hirew a local tree-man for the job. He had been the city forester for many years before going into business for himself, and we already knew and respected him. He really knew and understood trees, we thought.

When he heard what had happened, and what sort of close call we had both had, he just said in his laconic New-England way: "You know, those old trees usually fall considerately!"

"Considerately"--yes, that was just the right word!

1) I've heard about it but I haven't looked into it.

2) Mae is a form of the verb "to be" in Welsh, which has a very unusual sentence structure. It means roughly "there is." Y in Welsh is never pronounced as in "food," it's always the "obscure" U as in "Arthur."

3) The bright green also works well.

4) One of the essentials of the practice is to learn to tap into energy flows outside yourself.

5 and 6) Try it and see how it works.

7) It's the same center. Try feeling it in three dimensions, extending it forward and back.

comments.title: Banishing etiquette
comments.content:
Hi, JMG- I'm a couple of Magic Mondays behind in my reading, and today came upon the thread from two weeks back where you mentioned that you wouldn't recommend performing a straight Golden Dawn LBRP in the woods, for instance, because it starts from a premise of banishing the element of Earth, which is not exactly courteous to any local nature spirits. Having done exactly that a number of times over the last couple of years, I immediately felt retroactively rather mortified. Hoping to avoid similar missteps in the future:

1. If performing a standard LRM-style daily practice while in a public place outdoors (LRP, MP, discursive meditation), would you advise using the invoking Pentagrams rather than the banishing ones, and find time at another point in the day to do the banishing ritual?

2. In the same thread, you mention that the CGD LBRP was designed to be less hard-edged than the classical version, and that the SOP is gentler yet. Out of curiosity, where would the BRC (Banishing Ritual of the Cross) you've posted here a couple of times fall on this same continuum? And would you say it's broadly compatible with the system found in LRM/POW/COP (for example, could one perform the BRC to banish in a situation where one didn't want to banish Earth per se, then the LIRP and Middle Pillar), or would using the two together make for an awkward mix? I've used the BRC from time to time with what seem like good results, but I've thus far made a point of doing so at a different time of day, not as part of a sequence of other Golden Dawn-style practices (I asked you about this some time ago, and you responded that use of the BRC in the morning, followed by an LIRP in the evening seemed workable...)

Thanks much!
-NPM

replies.title: Re: Banishing etiquette
replies.content:
1) That would be worth trying.

2) I haven't experimented with it, since I'm not a Christian, and so I don't have any direct knowledge. My guess is that it would be somewhat gentler than either LBRP, but how it would compare to the SoP is anyone's guess. A.E. Waite's Golden Dawn order used a very similar ritual in place of the LBRP, so it's probably entirely compatible with the rest of the GD system.

comments.title: Back Again Another Week
comments.content:
Hello, Mr. Greer.
I have three queries for you this week. Thank you for your time and patience in advance.

1) Is it possible to create a Sphere of Protection by accident? When I was worried about psychic invaders, I started playing music that I knew parasites don't like on headphones. In my secondary vision, a thick membrane of energy (qi, the Force,etc.) formed a protective bubble in the room, pressing out to the edges. What is this?
NOTE: Since then, I have practiced until I am able to create this energy bubble on my own, without the use of music, although music helps make it stronger.

2)In my meditations, energy hasn't been flowing out of me as wildly, and I haven't needed to direct it into my wand or the ammonite. Instead, the energy turns white and forms a pyramid around my body. Is there a reason for this? What is it doing?
NOTE: The wand and the ammonite are doing fine, though, they are just not needing to be "fed" anymore. It's as though we are working together entirely.

3) When I was in the Unitarian Church, from ages 12-13, we were all obliged to work in a group called Coming of Age. The Unitarian Church has no setting between off and high on magic -- either it doesn’t exist, only science does; or we were shoved into magical work without any kind of protection or preparation whatsoever. For example, we had several vision quests, and on one, we did a guided meditation to meet our three Spirit Guides.
The first one, for me, was a balding monk in a dark red robe, holding a silver spindle with thread on it made of words.
The second was a creature I called the EarthThark (he only had two arms, not four), who gave me a magical knife.
The third was an irritable-looking Kindly One with blue skin, who handed me a rock he picked off the ground.
The monk smiled at me but was silent, the Kindly One said he was there to fill a quota, but the EarthThark was far different.
There was this feeling of love that came from him, a love that I've never felt from a human and know a human could never produce, because it wasn't human, it was so overpowering, this love, that I started crying from the intensity of it. I knew I didn't deserve it but it didn't matter, there was only love. I think that when people talk about the fear/awe they have upon seeing angels, it's because of that intensity of love. It didn't matter who or what or how you were, there is only a love so intense and endless that it doesn't exist on Earth, it can't.
I have been told by other pagans that pursuing further contact with the EarthThark is dangerous, because spirit guides shouldn’t “love bomb” you, and that he’s probably not a spirit guide anyway, because of that. Plus a lot of other very nasty implications, enough that I haven’t dared contact him again. I would like your opinion on this matter, and whether I should investigate all three, just the EarthThark, or none at all?

replies.title: Re: Back Again Another Week
replies.content:
1) That's quite a common experience. Your aura was thickening at the outer edges and becoming resistant.

2) I have no idea. Have you considered journaling? You're the one that's doing it -- subconsciously, perhaps, but at some level you know what you're up to.

3) Your pagan friends are jealous. I'd encourage you to follow up on the contacts.

replies.title: Re: Back Again Another Week
replies.content:
3a) I know what I want to do with my magical practice, but I do not know whether it falls more along the lines of ceremonial magic or natural magic. What I want to do is protect people (and myself!), and help them, if I can. The first magic I ever did was a charm to protect my father from the influence of Lilith, and learning to protect my soul from being stolen.

The pagan circles I run in outside of your blog tend to hold that any angelic magic is immediately suspect, with Core Shamanism and Ceremonial Magic being equally suspect, albeit in different ways. My anthropology professors have defined shamanism as the magic of recovery and curing -- finding lost souls, healing damaged ones, protecting the community and clients. That's the sort of thing I want to do, but the angelic and ceremonial magic texts I've read are the only examples of magical instruction books that actually assume you want to DO SOMETHING with it. Give me Budge over a hundred thousand weak-tea pagan outfits.

replies.title: Re: Back Again Another Week
replies.content: Unfortunately Harnerian "core shamanism" doesn't give you a lot of tools for action, and real shamanism -- no, I'm not a Harner fan -- requires access to a living tradition, which is not an easy thing if you don't come from a cultural background that includes it. The great advantage of ceremonial magic is that it's right out there, available for anyone who's willing to do the work. I know that a lot of Neopagans shudder and back away from ceremonial magic; it's up to you to decide whether you're willing to make up your own mind and commit yourself to the years of hard solitary work that will be needed to give you the ability to do what you want to do.

replies.title: Re: Back Again Another Week
replies.content:
Yeah, Harner is absolutely...he's a mixed bag as an anthropologist, and a net negative when it comes to paganism. He's no Castenada, but that's damning with faint praise.

Thank you for your help this week, as always.

comments.content:
JMG,

Since I'm a DA student, I'm trying to learn to think in DA terms instead of the terminology I normally use.

1) Are the halls of hollow forms the same thing as the etheric plane? They're both described as being either just below the astral plane or else as the lowest subset of the astral plane, but my impression of the etheric plane is that it's simply where life force operates, while the passage on the Halls of Hollow Forms gives me the impression that it's a kind of sediment layer unrelated to the etheric bodies of living beings.

2) At first glance, the body of vitality seems to correspond to the etheric body, the animal body to the astral body, and the magic mirror of the cosmos to the aura, but some of the description makes me think that maybe that's not right and the animal form is only present in those about to be reborn as animals. Is the animal form a part of the astral body that is present in everyone, a part that is present in everyone but that only takes the form of an animal in those going to be reborn as animals, or a part that only exists for those about to be reborn as animals?

Thank you!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) No. The Halls of the Hollow Forms are at the lowest level of the astral plane.

2) The cydfil or animal form isn't present in everyone. It only exists in those who are heading for an animal rebirth. (Some sources suggest that animals who are going to be born as human beings the next time around have human forms, which look like children.)

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: Do you have any advice to help develop a sense of perspective as to what is and is not worth getting upset over?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Sure. Can you think of a situation in which getting upset about something will actually accomplish anything positive and meaningful? If so, let yourself get upset over that. In any situation where getting upset won't do anything, or won't do anything worthwhile, don't bother.

(This is a version of the basic Stoic approach: "Can you do something about it? Then it matters. If you can't do anything about it, it doesn't." Harsh, but it works.)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: One trick I've found for when getting upset doesn't help but it's hard to stop (oh hello anxiety disorder) is to set aside a designated Get Upset Time. I pick fifteen minutes and tell myself "okay, you're going to be sad about this breakup/scared about your parents' aging/whatever as hard as you can until the timer goes off, and then you'll stop." After the first time or two, I usually don't need the whole 15.
